headline: Clemson edges Boston College in defensive slugfest despite BC's yardage advantage
body: James Cortez delivered when it mattered most, completing 19 of 30 passes for 252 yards and engineering two scoring drives that proved just enough to hold off Boston College's more prolific offensive attack. Clemson's defense bent but refused to break, allowing 405 total yards yet surrendering only 20 points while forcing a critical turnover late in the contest.

Boston College's Matt Leavitt was the more efficient passer, completing 27 of 47 attempts for 292 yards with two touchdown throws. The Eagles gained 113 rushing yards to Clemson's 114, essentially matching the Tigers on the ground, yet couldn't find the finishing touch in the red zone when opportunities arose. Clemson's Adams Jr punched in a rushing touchdown that would ultimately prove the margin of victory.

The statistical imbalance tells the story of a game where field position and turnover luck favored the Tigers. Clemson converted limited opportunities into points while Boston College's volume advantage translated into only a one-point deficit. This was a defensive win for Clemson, not a statement of offensive dominance, and defensive tackle Mickens anchored the line with eight tackles to keep BC's explosive tendencies in check.
turningPoint: Clemson's forced fumble late in the fourth quarter halted Boston College's momentum and denied the Eagles a chance to reclaim the lead in the final minutes.

unsungHero.name: Marcus Mickels
unsungHero.why: Eight tackles and relentless interior presence kept Boston College's rhythm disrupted all afternoon, yet Cortez gets the Player of the Game nod for managing the win—Mickels did the invisible work that made Clemson's defense functional.
